free_text: Excitation functions for production of medically
relevant radioisotopes in deuteron irradiations of
Pr and Tm targets
Ohtshuki -> Ohtsuki
Oxides of Tm and Pr were prepared by sedimentation
technique on Al or Ni backings.
Irradiations were performed at the external beams of
the Tohoku University (Sendai) and Vrije Universiteit
Brussels (VUB) cyclotrons.
The Tm2O3:
25-35 microns in Tm equivalent for Sendai experiments;
15-60 microns thick for VUB experiments, purity 99.9%.
The Pr6O11:
250-60 lm thick in Pr equivalent, purity 99.5%.
Al (100 microns) and Ti (12.5 microns) monitor foils
were used. The stacks were mounted in a Faraday cup
like target holder equipped with a suppressor of
secondary electrons. The energy of the incident
deuteron beams was up to 40 MeV at Sendai and 20 MeV
at VUB, respectively. The beam current: of 90-200 nA.
Independent and cumulative cross sections were
determined.
Downshifts of 0.5-2 MeV in nominal incident energy and
corrections in particle flux of a few percent result
were necessary to apply.
updated web version
The monitoring method allows estimating the
uncertainty on the primary beam energy at +-0.2 MeV.
The uncertainty on the average energy in each foil is,
however, continuously rising through the stack and
attains +-1 MeV for the last foil due to cumulative
possible variations on target thickness and energy
degradation.
The included experimental uncertainties are:
* number of target nuclei including non-uniformity
of the deposits,
* incident particle flux,
* peak area including statistical errors on counts,
* detector efficiency,
* gamma ray abundance and branching rate data.
A total uncertainty of 10-15% is obtained as positive
square root of the quadratic sum of the individual
sources. Possible additional uncertainties due to non
linear effects of half-lives and waiting time were not
accounted for.
Decay and spectrometric characteristics of the
investigated radionuclides were taken from Nudat2.
Thick target production yields were calculated
from the fitted experimental cross sections.
S.Takacs, 2009.
